how_it_works: Provider WebSockets (Deepgram STT, Cartesia/Deepgram TTS) run one long-lived socket through WebSocketConnection (packages/voice-ws/src/index.ts:96). On unexpected close, onClose (index.ts:266) fires onConnectionLost and tryReconnect (index.ts:293): a quick-failure breaker (re-open-then-die within minStableMs, maxQuickFailures times -> giveUp, index.ts:300) guards bad-key loops, otherwise it loops openSocket + verify with equal-jitter backoff (waitForRetryDelay, retry.ts:79). Reconnect config is re-injected because url() is rebuilt each attempt (deepgram index.ts:120). Replay-on-reconnect (index.ts:130-163) buffers ONLY frames that failed because the socket was not open (so a frame that reached an open socket is never replayed -> no duplicate speech) and flushReplay re-sends them in onOpen. retry.ts adds a low-latency provider profile (250ms base / 10s cap) plus an outage profile (4s floor / 10s cap), selected via readProviderRetryConfig. Graceful drain lives in packages/voice-server-websocket/src/index.ts:364: close({graceful}) snapshots a per-socket gracefulCloseRegistry and calls drainAndClose with a deadline, force-terminating wedged sockets at the deadline (tested in graceful-drain.test.ts). SIGTERM/SIGINT is wired only in the two example server scripts via installGracefulShutdown (websocket-lifecycle.ts:67). Watchdogs (voice-agent-session-util.ts:78) cover STT force-finalize, VAQI missed-response, TTS stall, and a NEW input-cadence watchdog that re-arms on every inbound audio packet (voice-agent-session.ts:552) and on stall emits a metric + recoverable pipeline.error. The new ProviderFallback core (provider-fallback.ts) round-robins providers, banishes a failing one to an unavailable set, and runs self-rescheduling background recovery probes -- but it is only exported, never instantiated by any live provider.

findings.title: Input-cadence watchdog emits only a metric + error packet, no recovery action; disabled by default
findings.evidence: On stall it does only: bus.push(Route.Background, make.metric(cid,"input.cadence_stall_ms",...)) and bus.push(Route.Critical, {kind:"pipeline.error", component:"pipeline", category:NetworkTimeout, cause:new Error("inbound audio stalled"), isRecoverable:true}). The consumer handleComponentError -> maybeSpeakErrorFallback returns immediately for any non-llm component (`if (pkt.component !== "llm") return;`), so a "pipeline" cadence stall triggers NO recovery (no carrier silence/keepalive, no reconnect). And it is off by default: `this.inputCadenceTimeoutMs = config.inputCadenceTimeoutMs ?? 0;` with `scheduleInputCadenceWatchdog` early-returning when the timeout is 0.
findings.recommendation: Take an actual recovery action on cadence stall (send carrier silence/keepalive frame to the transport, or probe/reset the inbound path) rather than only emitting a metric+error, and ship a non-zero default (the bridge suggests ~500ms) or document why it is opt-out. As written the watchdog satisfies VE-06.4's metric half but not its 'recovery action' half.
findings.checklist_ref: VE-06 bridge VE-06.4; PRODUCTION-CHECKLIST §7 (watchdogs emit recovery actions, not only metrics)

findings.title: New ProviderFallback core is never wired into any live STT/TTS provider
findings.evidence: grep across packages for ProviderFallback/FallbackProvider outside the file and its test returns only `packages/voice/src/index.ts:135: export { ProviderFallback, ... }`. No provider package (deepgram STT, cartesia/deepgram TTS) constructs a ProviderFallback. The class is exercised only by provider-fallback.test.ts with string stub providers.
findings.recommendation: Either wire ProviderFallback into at least one real provider path (STT or TTS) with concrete adapters + a probe, or mark VE-06.3/R-07 explicitly PARTIAL in the checklist. Until wired, the 'STT and TTS fallback adapters emit availability changes' acceptance is met only by a unit test, not by the running engine -- a real outage today still surfaces as a hard failure, not a fallback.
findings.checklist_ref: VE-06 bridge VE-06.3/VE-06.6; handoff R-07 PARTIAL

findings.title: Graceful drain does not gate new-session admission; new calls are accepted then killed mid-startup
findings.evidence: The connection handler `wsServer.on("connection", (socket, request) => { void runWebSocketConnection(...); })` is never gated on the `closing` flag. shouldAbort inside the host is `() => socketClosed || startupTimedOut` (transport-host.ts:176) -- it has no notion of a server-level drain. close() snapshots gracefulCloseRegistry.values() at line 370 BEFORE any new session registers (registration happens in wireSession at index.ts:235), so a connection arriving during the up-to-10s drain window builds a full session (createSession -> STT/TTS/LLM startup) and is then abruptly terminated at line 386, never drained.
findings.recommendation: Reject (or 1013/503) new upgrades once `closing` is true, e.g. check the flag in the connection handler before runWebSocketConnection, and add a drain-time admission test (none exists in graceful-drain.test.ts). VE-06.5 acceptance explicitly requires 'new-session admission gated during drain'.
findings.checklist_ref: VE-06 bridge VE-06.5 ('new-session admission gated during drain')

findings.title: Replay frames lost when reconnect socket opens but fails verify()
findings.evidence: flushReplay() runs inside onOpen (index.ts:247) and clears pendingReplay (index.ts:153-154) the instant the socket reports open. tryReconnect then calls `await this.socket.verify(VERIFY_TIMEOUT_MS)` (index.ts:321); if verify returns false the loop falls through to backoff and re-opens, disposing that socket. The frames flushed into the unverified socket are gone -- they were drained from the buffer but the socket proved dead, so they are neither received by the provider nor re-buffered for the next attempt.
findings.recommendation: Defer flushReplay until AFTER verify() succeeds in tryReconnect (move the flush out of onOpen, or re-buffer on verify failure). The current ordering breaks the replay guarantee precisely in the verify-failure case the verify step exists to catch.
findings.checklist_ref: VE-06 bridge VE-06.2 ('failed in-flight frame is replayed or explicit metric says not replayable')

findings.title: Fallback banishes a provider on ANY error, including one-off timeouts and aborts, with no recoverable/fatal distinction
findings.evidence: send() catches every error uniformly: `catch (err) { lastError = err; this.markUnavailable(provider); }` and markUnavailable adds the provider to `unavailable` until a probe succeeds. The AbortSignal.timeout(attemptTimeoutMs) firing (a transient slow response) is treated identically to a hard outage, and there is no use of the existing categorizeSttError/isRecoverable logic to decide whether banishment is warranted.
findings.recommendation: Distinguish a transient/timeout failure (retry same provider once, or short cooldown) from a fatal one (banish + probe), reusing error-handler.ts categorization, so a single slow turn does not exile the primary provider for a full recoveryProbeIntervalMs.
findings.checklist_ref: VE-06 bridge 'Risks & edge cases'; PRODUCTION-CHECKLIST §7 graceful degradation

summary: The reconnect/replay core is genuinely solid: replay buffers ONLY provably-unsent frames (no duplicate speech), config is re-injected via a fresh url() each attempt, the quick-failure breaker stops bad-key loops, and the retry profiles correctly keep a ~125-250ms first reconnect while allowing a 4s/10s outage profile. The graceful drain mechanism is well-built and well-tested (graceful + forced + multi-client, real sockets/codes in graceful-drain.test.ts). The strongest gaps are not in that machinery but in completeness vs the VE-06 acceptance criteria: (1) the input-cadence watchdog only emits a metric + a non-actionable recoverable pipeline.error -- no recovery action -- and is disabled by default (inputCadenceTimeoutMs ?? 0); (2) the new ProviderFallback is exported and unit-tested but never instantiated by any live provider, so a real STT/TTS outage still hard-fails; (3) graceful drain accepts new sessions during the drain window then kills them mid-startup (no admission gate, untested). One narrower correctness bug: flushReplay runs in onOpen before verify(), so frames are lost if a reconnected socket opens but fails verification. SIGTERM is correctly wired (example server entrypoints) reusing the existing drain. Recommend gating admission during drain, defaulting/actioning the cadence watchdog, and wiring (or explicitly de-scoping) ProviderFallback before claiming VE-06 done.
